excerpt_en: So today being Saturday, I held a walking event for the first time in two weeks. The number of reservations was 40. So how many would actually show up? The result was 20 people joining us! That means about 50% of the reservations actually came through,
body_en:
So today being Saturday,

I held a walking event for the first time in two weeks.

The number of reservations was 40.

So how many would actually show up?

The result was 20 people joining us!

That means about 50% of the reservations actually came through!

Simply put, I'm happy that 20 people showed up.

Along with that,

handling registration by myself was quite a challenge! lol

5 euros per person, 4 euros for students.

I collect the money and record names on the roster.

Doing this 20 times,

even at 1 minute per person, it takes 20 minutes! lol

That said,

I feel like this is something that only gets faster with practice.

Registration done, the walking event kicked off!

It goes like this:

we switch partners every 10 minutes,

enjoying conversations along the way!

Since it's 60 minutes,

you end up chatting with about 5 to 6 people.

The paths are narrow,

so one-on-one conversations are the norm.

This means zero people who don't talk! lol

I've forcefully created an environment where conversation is inevitable.

After about 60 minutes of walking,

we headed to a cafe.

It's a Starbucks,

and the basement room is really spacious,

so it's super convenient!

That said, with 20 people buying at Starbucks,

we're contributing massively to their sales. lol

If this were a privately-owned shop,

I could send customers their way,

which would really help their revenue.

But for now, I haven't found one,

and there aren't many places that can comfortably fit 20+ people, so this is it for now.

From here, it's a free-form social session!

You can keep talking to someone you already spoke with,

or you can talk to someone you haven't met yet.

There are no particular restrictions,

it's totally free.

I thought about setting conversation topics at first, but I decided it's better for everyone to just talk about whatever they want.

When I asked everyone for feedback,

"I was looking for a social event without alcohol."

"I love walking and talking one-on-one."

"It was so much fun, I'll definitely come again."

"Next time I'll bring my son."

Those were some of the comments I received.

I'm truly grateful!!

And so,

the whole thing wrapped up in about two hours.

So many new connections were made.

Of course, not just with me -- the participants were exchanging contact info with each other too, which was awesome.

An event where you get healthy by walking AND make friends --

that's pretty unique to our events!

Being able to create an environment where so many people are happy is incredibly rewarding, and I'm constantly learning, so it's a huge learning experience for me too.

Of course, there's a lot of work involved --

prep work,

managing the app group,

promoting the events, and so on.

So I don't do this as volunteer work;

I properly charge a fee

to make the operation sustainable.

*I'm also aware of the value of my own labor

FOMUS Online Event

The walking event ended at 12:30,

I cleaned up at Starbucks,

and from 1:00 PM

I held a FOMUS online event!

I talked about my activities in Ireland

and about the Medoc Marathon.

The participants were

the Medoc Marathon members,

Yuri-san, a FOMUS masu model,

and Professor Kawamukai.

Including me, it was a small report session with just 5 people.

This time, it wasn't about having a large number, so personally I was fine with this!

I prepared presentation materials designed so that even someone unfamiliar with FOMUS's history could follow along.

Yusaku, the college student who joined the Medoc Marathon,

I met him through this year's Philippines trip, so I hadn't had a chance to tell him about the journey FOMUS has been on -- so the timing was perfect.

And we were also able to discuss

at what point each participant had connected with us,

which was something only possible because of the small group.

Plus, except for Professor Kawamukai,

everyone had met at the Hakone event,

and Yusaku and Yuri-san

had even met at the Sagi Island painting event. lol

The connections among people deeply involved with FOMUS -- or rather, with me -- are getting stronger!

After we finished the online discussion,

it turned into a time to listen to Professor Kawamukai,

and I received tons of advice!

This too was only possible because of the small group.

Once again, the connections built through all my activities so far

are truly something that lasts a lifetime.

While working in Ireland,

normally people might think, "What about Japan?" But by consistently putting out information,

I'm able to convey that MaSU is working in Ireland to expand FOMUS,

and that keeps us connected.

Sharing information is really important!!

When I Tried to Go Home

After the online meeting,

I had a quick lunch,

and while wandering around

looking for a place to hold an art exhibition,

I happened to run into some friends!!

They were heading to the Irish version of Don Quijote, and I was curious too, so I tagged along! lol

I bought some toothpaste I'd been wanting

and some hair gel!

After that, we went to the BAR I use for my events.

We chatted about all sorts of things there.

In the end, today turned out to have

three social gatherings:

the Walking Event,

the FOMUS Online Meetup,

and the Japanese Residents in Dublin hangout!

lol

Connections are truly fascinating,

and it reminded me once again that none of us live alone!

Tomorrow there's another walking event waiting.

Reservations are at 100.

How many will actually show up?

I can't wait!

title_en: An International Exchange Event with Insanely Good Reviews
excerpt_en: Life in Ireland has entered its 8th week! On Sundays, I host two events: a walking event in the morning and a card game "SILVA" event in the evening. Reservations had at one point exceeded 100,
body_en:
Life in Ireland has entered its 8th week!

On Sundays,

**a walking event in the morning

and a card game "SILVA" event in the evening**

-- I'm hosting two events.

Reservations had at one point exceeded 100,

but the actual turnout was 20 people.

This was as expected.

Since it's not a food-related event,

even if cancellations come in,

there's almost no risk, which is really nice.

#The events I held in Japan were mostly food-related so they were tough

I've hosted these so many times now

that the process is perfect!

I have participants pay the fee first,

then hand them a slip of paper with an alphabet letter and a number.

Previously, I made proper printed cards, but collecting them was a pain, and everyone treated them as trash, so they couldn't be reused for future events. This time I just handwrote them.

As long as you can read the number and letter, that's all that matters

-- that's the approach.

This system

works perfectly every single time.

However, someone who gave me advice after the event said that European participants also want to talk to other Europeans.

This was just by chance,

but probably the Japanese students ended up having a lot of conversation opportunities.

I might need to think about the seating arrangement

so that Europeans can also interact with each other.

That said, the ratio of Japanese to non-Japanese participants constantly changes, so it's hard to say for sure right now.

Japanese participants also want to talk to people from abroad,

but when they come with friends,

the Japanese ratio inevitably goes up...

Why Do People Show Up?

So the question is, "Why do we get so many reservations?"

When I ask event participants,

they say that when you search

"Dublin" and "making friends"

on the Meetup app,

my group apparently shows up in the top 3.

This is just my guess,

but I think it's because the review scores

-- which only participants can write --

are high.

During just the walking portion,

I don't have time

to properly talk to everyone,

but after the 1-hour walk, I also hold a social session at a cafe.

There, I make sure to talk to every single person.

#Gotta get the word out about FOMUS

#Also properly thanking everyone for coming

I've never once asked anyone

to "write a review,"

but when they go to leave a rating,

my face probably pops into their mind (hopefully),

so the scores end up being high,

and as a result, the event gets recognized

as a high-quality one. lol

The same goes for the card game event.

"Everyone plays a card game that nobody has ever played before"

-- in a way,

cooperative play naturally happens as people figure out the rules together,

and it brings everyone closer!

#SILVA takes a little time to learn

I'm really savoring the beauty of entertainment

that has a low translation barrier. lol

As for future challenges,

since I, a Japanese person, am the organizer,

the Japanese ratio could increase too much

and throw off the balance.

#Japanese students in Dublin have found out about us

#Word of mouth among students spreads fast

#Maybe because they're close in age

If that happens, I'll need more non-Japanese participants, so I'll try various things with social media to address that!

Card Game Event in the Evening

This time, people who had repeatedly come to the walking events decided to join the SILVA card game event.

Since they come to events multiple times,

I end up talking with them multiple times,

so we get closer,

and having them come to the card game event on top of that is truly wonderful.

As for explaining the card game rules

and the card effects,

I think I can convey most of it in English,

so I feel like things can accelerate from here.

And so,

week 8 in Ireland continues,

and I'll be out there more than anyone, absorbing everything I can!!

excerpt_en: Having Time to Spare. Today I had no online meetings for a change, and had some free time. While attending class at school, there was an announcement. Free afternoon English classes were being offered! Normally afternoon classes cost money,
body_en:
Having Time to Spare

Today I had no online meetings for a change,

and had some free time.

While attending class at school,

there was an announcement.

Free afternoon English classes were being offered!

Normally afternoon classes cost money,

but these were free because they doubled as training for the teachers.

I happened to have an opening in my schedule,

so of course I joined!

#Always jump in when there's a chance for self-improvement

To be honest, even though it was teacher training,

the content was English study,

so it was an environment where I inevitably spent more time using English.

In the end, I spent about 2 extra hours

on English study compared to usual.

One of my goals in coming to Ireland

is to be able to have basic conversations

in English

by the time I turn 30,

so I'll keep studying English diligently.

Dinner with Japanese Residents in Dublin

Today I was invited to a small dinner gathering with fellow Japanese people.

Everyone else had been living and working in Dublin

for about 3 to 7 years.

#I've been here less than 2 months

As the newcomer,

I run in the Medoc Marathon,

organize events,

do photography, promote masu, and other things --

they found my unconventional activities interesting

and invited me along.

Naturally, they know Dublin 100 times better than I do

and have extensive connections,

so I learned a ton.

I also shared the challenges I'm currently facing

and was able to exchange ideas,

which moved the FOMUS brand expansion in Ireland

another step forward!

*A Note About Corporate Workers

There were 4 of us this time,

and everyone except me works for a company.

Proper major companies, too.

They're people far more talented than me.

However, one thing I found curious was that,

despite being so talented,

the conversation was full of complaints about things they wanted their bosses to fix.

I guess workplace relationship issues exist everywhere.

Personally, I think talking about people who aren't there is pointless, and since you can't control other people, I thought it was a rather meaningless conversation as I listened.

*Can't write that publicly though

But I suppose that venting time

is necessary for them.

For my part,

I expected the conversation to be more like,

"I'm working on this next project!"

"Wanna do something together?"

But since everyone is a company employee,

the difference between people who receive a salary

and people who need to generate money

exists even across national borders.

I'm absolutely not criticizing them --

I just came to understand

the difference more clearly.

Also, one of the women at dinner

lived in the same area as me,

so we chatted a lot on the way home.

During the dinner itself,

she hadn't been the main speaker much,

so this opportunity to talk revealed that

she had an incredibly interesting background.

She's younger than me,

has already been active here for 6 years,

and is a great fit for FOMUS.

By the end of the year or early next year,

we might be able to cook up something interesting!

Five days ago,

my homestay location suddenly changed,

and because of that, we ended up going the same direction home and had a deep conversation.

You never know what life has in store!

The encounter with these people also came through my exchange events,

so the pledge I made strongly when leaving Japan --

"Take more action than anyone, put in more effort than anyone" --

continuing that for these past 2 months

is what produced this result.

That said, I haven't accomplished anything yet,

so I'll keep building steadily and produce results!!

excerpt_en: 9/18 After class, I jumped straight into a FOMUS Japan meeting. Currently in Japan, a new project is getting started, and we're holding regular meetings to keep it moving. It's more of a project meeting than a FOMUS community thing,
body_en:
9/18

After class,

I jumped straight into a FOMUS Japan meeting.

Currently in Japan,

a new project is getting started,

and we're holding regular meetings to keep it moving.

It's more of a project meeting

than a FOMUS community thing,

so it's a small-group discussion.

SILVA Event

It's Wednesday, so from 6 PM to 7:30 PM

I host a card game "SILVA"

event.

Weekday events have fewer people,

so we can take our time chatting before starting, and I personally love this time.

We chatted for about 20 minutes

before I explained SILVA.

This being the 5th time,

I've gotten used to explaining it in English.

It's actually easier to teach while playing.

What made me happy was that

someone who came two weeks ago

brought a friend along.

On top of that,

that friend is going to study abroad in Japan next year!

She found out I was Japanese

and that's why she was brought along.

I'm truly grateful.

Plus, I've spent the past 3 years

traveling all around Japan,

so I know a lot of great things about the country.

When I was a student,

I didn't know Japan that well,

so when asked for recommendations,

I could only suggest

the usual spots.

Sometimes

the other person even knew more than me.

But now, it's different.

Thanks to traveling around Japan quite a bit,

I can speak from both knowledge and experience.

She and I agreed to meet up again another day.

*Since today was a card game event

Once again, I was able to create a space for exchange

while also getting people to know about SILVA!

Off to a Japanese Meetup

From there, I headed straight to

the weekly Japanese meetup.

Today, the woman I met at the small

Japanese dinner party the other day

had told me in advance that someone who runs a Japanese restaurant in Dublin would be there.

*She messaged me during the card game

I'd already planned to go,

but knowing ahead of time

put me more at ease.

When I arrived, the restaurant owner was already there,

and we had a great conversation.

I wasn't trying to pitch masu,

so I put all my energy into getting them to know "MaSU" as a person first.

Everyone around had been in Dublin for 3 to 8+ years, and I could feel the deep bonds they shared.

That's exactly why I need to find my way in

from a completely different angle.

That said, I also need the people around them

to get to know MaSU,

so when some of them slipped away from the meetup event

to go somewhere else,

I tagged along.

Obviously, everyone around me had deep connections,

and from my perspective,

it was outside my comfort zone.

The old me

might have held back and not gone,

but now, none of that matters -- I just go for it. lol

There, it was more casual chatting

than business talk,

and I got a feel for the atmosphere.

I managed to leave an impression with the veterans: "There's this new guy called MaSU. Apparently he does stuff with masu and organizes events."

To deepen relationships from here,

I need to talk to them many more times,

so I'll keep attending these meetups.

excerpt_en: In the evening, I attended an event called CultureNight held in Ireland. Culture Night is an annual cultural event held across European countries and other regions.
body_en:
In the evening, I attended an event called CultureNight held in Ireland.

Culture Night is

an annual cultural event held across European countries and other regions.

Usually held in September, museums, theaters, cultural facilities, and other institutions open their doors for free at night, offering a wide variety of cultural and artistic programs.

The purpose is to showcase local culture and provide opportunities for citizens to share cultural experiences.

It's held on a large scale especially in countries like Ireland and Denmark, featuring music, performances, exhibitions, workshops, and all sorts of events.

I only found out about it a few days before,

so unfortunately, events with limited capacity

were already full and I couldn't get in.

However, there were plenty of free-entry events, so I joined in alongside the locals.

Honestly, I didn't know what to attend, so I just decided to hit up events starting from the earliest time slots.

First up, a university.

When I arrived, there were already about 100 adults, and we got a presentation about the university.

However, the topic was so unfamiliar

that I could barely understand anything.

I had tickets for the next event,

so I left early.

Next, about a 10-minute walk away, a church.

There was a music event here.

It was packed,

but since I had gotten tickets in advance, I was able to get in.

As for the performance,

I honestly couldn't tell if it was good or not.

Next, St. Patrick's Cathedral.

I was supposed to be able to hear a choir and other performances,

but by the time I got there, they had already finished,

so I only got to tour the inside.

To be honest, after seeing the Sagrada Familia,

every other church feels smaller in scale,

and I wish I could do something about that. lol

After that, I walked about 20 minutes

to a chocolate factory.

It said you could see various art pieces there, but it was way smaller than I expected.

Last stop, a club.

They had drag queen

performances going on.

It was only 8 PM,

but tons of people had gathered

and the place was buzzing.

Personally, I'm not a fan of noisy places, but there were loads of people there, and people gather for a reason, so I did some research.

There were couples,

groups of just girls, groups of just guys.

Because drinking culture is so strong here,

events like this

naturally attract large crowds.

I watched the performances with a beer in hand too.

That said,

I was alone, so I left without talking to anyone.

Events like these are best enjoyed with friends.

And so,

I attended this once-a-year event.

I really wanted to connect more with the local community, but it didn't go as smoothly as I'd hoped,

which was a point of reflection.

Maybe it wasn't that kind of event to begin with, but I can't help feeling like I could've done a bit more.

The Last Dinner

So, today marks exactly 2 months since I came to Ireland.

Which means my homestay is over.

Originally, I was supposed to stay at the same homestay for the full 2 months, but due to the host's circumstances I had to move twice,

making this already my 3rd place! lol

On the bright side, I got to actually experience different Irish lifestyles.

And I learned that homestays

are truly a gamble.

Some places are great,

some are not.

Location aside,

there were comfortable places

and not-so-comfortable ones.

Actually, this 3rd place

was honestly the best.

Because there was no weird interference.

Of course, we'd chat during meals,

but otherwise I was free.

At the first place, meal times were fixed,

I couldn't enter the living room outside of those times,

and the food was practically slop.

I had to skip lunch every day and use hunger as a spice just to eat it.

The second place had good food

and was generally nice,

but even though I had my own room,

they'd enter without permission

and tell me to clean up.

I didn't think I was being messy,

but the father was strict about rules,

and they even set specific times for when I could shower.

These kinds of restrictions

were tough for someone in their late 20s like me.

So, homestays come with

both advantages and disadvantages.

But that's all part of the homestay experience,

and it was a good learning experience.

Starting tomorrow, for one month,

I'm moving to a place I found on Airbnb.

Honestly, I chose it based on price,

so I haven't done a viewing, and I don't know if it's clean or not.

The reviews aren't that great either,

so I'm a little nervous. lol

That said, it's only a one-month rental,

and since I'm out all day,

it's just a place to sleep anyway.

And from here on,

since I won't be in a homestay anymore,

I'll have to do the housework myself.

More freedom comes with

more things to handle.

So, what'll happen?

I'm looking forward to it!

excerpt_en: Today marks exactly 2 full months since I came to Ireland. That means 8 weeks have passed. So, my homestay life is now over. Starting today, it's a share house. But before that, I had a walking event in the morning!
body_en:
Today marks exactly

2 full months since I came to Ireland.

That means 8 weeks have passed.

So,

my homestay life is now over.

Starting today, it's a share house.

But,

before that -- a morning walking event!

We had fewer people today,

so instead of walking along the river, we walked through the city streets.

This was fun in its own way!

As for the event format,

if I pair Japanese participants only with non-Japanese,

the non-Japanese participants don't get to interact with each other.

So I intentionally allowed Japanese-to-Japanese pairings this time.

Rather than prioritizing the satisfaction of the Japanese participants,

the goal is for the local non-Japanese people to have a good time.

The Difficulty of Collecting Data

Today, out of 20 reservations, 6 people showed up.

In other words,

30% of reservations came through.

70%

registered but didn't show up.

This fluctuates with every event,

and on top of that, the number of reservations varies each time too.

Until now, over 50 people would reserve,

and about 20 would come,

but this week, reservations were

only around 15 to 20 for both today and tomorrow.

That means

the number of people reserving dropped by over 50%.

This might be due to

the app's algorithm,

or maybe things have just settled down after the first month.

Personally,

if around 20 people consistently show up,

the event is really lively AND the revenue is promising.

That said, when it's a small group like today,

we can have deeper conversations,

so there are pros and cons to both.

There's still a lot I won't know

until I run a few more events.

That said, today we had 6 people,

and 2 of them

came from far away.

*1.5 hours one way, no less.

They said they don't usually attend meetups like this, and they told me they had a great time, so I was really glad.

*It would've been rough for them to travel all that way only to have a bad time

But if attendance drops below 5 consistently,

I'll change the event format

or possibly stop the events altogether.

Weekends are important for me too,

and I need to think about

where to spend my money and time.

I'll just keep doing trial and error on this front.

https://www.notion.so

Moving to the Share House

After the event wrapped up,

my 2-month homestay officially came to an end.

Time for the share house.

This time, knowing I can't stay in the same place for months on end,

I booked a 1-month place on Airbnb.

*3 months in one spot is pretty tough for me.

So,

I looked for the cheapest place possible.

Even so, it costs over 100,000 yen for 28 days.

And it's a share house.

In Japan, that money would get you

a really nice place.

When I lived in Tokyo,

I had my own apartment in Akasaka, Minato Ward,

so in Dublin,

the impression is: this amount of money gets you THIS level of housing.

Plus, after arriving,

despite the listing saying "single bed,"

it was a bunk bed...

At least I got the bottom bunk,

but I never expected to spend the next month in a 4-person room.

The house I chose

apparently has a ton of people living in it.

The living room is spacious,

so I watched people come and go,

and there are quite a few.

Probably over 10 people live here?

"This isn't what I was told!!"

I thought,

so I contacted the owner to confirm,

and got a strongly-worded email back saying,

"I've prepared a single bed."

"Don't send me pointless emails about this."

I really thought that was out of line.

But I took it as a lesson in what kind of adult I don't want to become,

and grew a little from it. lol

Since this is a paid article,

I'll leave it as is.

*By the way, I did file a complaint with Airbnb. lol

That said,

I've already paid over 100,000 yen,

and even if I leave this place,

everything else is even more expensive,

so I'll probably stay here for the one month until school ends.

After October 20th,

I haven't decided anything yet,

so whether I stay in Dublin

or travel to other countries, I don't know.

So that's how I moved into a share house.

Right away, I went to the nearby supermarket

and stocked up on food.

Pasta, tomato sauce, ham, bananas --

I usually don't eat breakfast,

but since getting hungry during class kills my concentration, I bought cereal and milk.

I also grabbed some muffins for snacks.

I cooked up some pasta right away,

and it was pretty good.

*The thick noodles were a nice touch

So yeah,

that's my report on moving into the share house.

Other than hotel dorms,

this is my first proper shared living arrangement

since my stay in Malaysia.

Well, I think I can enjoy this environment too,

and while there are plenty of inconveniences,

I'll try to make the most of it!

title_en: You Can't Start Without Stepping Up to the Plate
excerpt_en: As usual, I held a walking event in the morning! Today, perhaps by chance, the Japanese ratio was overwhelmingly low. Just 2 of us including me. Everyone came from different countries! Repeat participants showed up too!
body_en:
As usual,

I held a walking event in the morning!

Today, perhaps by chance,

the Japanese ratio was overwhelmingly low.

Just 2 of us including me.

Everyone came from different countries!

Repeat participants showed up too!

I'm grateful that

people who understand what the event is about keep coming,

so everything runs super smoothly!

Since we had about 10 people,

the line was compact enough that

instead of walking along the river,

we took a stroll through the city streets.

With 60 minutes of walking,

the format lets you interact with about 6 people.

This format is getting incredibly positive feedback.

It solves a lot of challenges.

The only thing left is getting Revolut --

an app like the Irish version of PayPay --

to connect properly, and then it'll be perfect.

The system of having dedicated 10-minute sessions

with each person, one after another,

has personally been a great fit for Dublin.

After that, everyone socialized at a cafe.

We chatted for about an hour,

and after the event, the whole group

decided to go for drinks in the late morning!

And everyone actually came along! lol

Everyone was having lunch,

but I had another meeting coming up, so I just had a beer.

My event was already over,

so I ducked out early.

For lunch, I met up with the girl I met at last week's card game event --

the one who's going to study abroad in Japan next year.

#My first Irish breakfast

She's Spanish,

loves Japanese culture and anime,

and since I'd also stayed in Spain before coming to Ireland, we had a lot to talk about!

#The benefits of visiting many countries

Even just having visited a place once,

it deepens the connection with someone

who has lived there,

so visiting various countries

and experiencing different cultures is really important.

After the lunch meetup,

I headed straight to the card game event!

This is also an event I organize.

And of course, I use "SILVA,"

which was created within the FOMUS community.

"I don't play any other card games" --

that's my stance, because the goal is

to promote the FOMUS brand

while facilitating international exchange.

6 people from 6 countries showed up,

and we had some intense card battles! lol

We had participants from both Russia and Ukraine,

and while their countries have their issues,

as individuals, none of that matters.

Everyone enjoyed SILVA together!

The venue was Starbucks,

and since the space was no longer available after 6 PM,

we weren't done playing yet,

so we relocated to the hotel venue where I host

the weekly Wednesday events and started up again.

In the end,

we played from 4 PM until past 7:30 PM! lol

Coincidences Are Fun

Normally, I would've gone home at that point,

but then I got a last-minute call. lol

A girl who went to the same language school in Cebu

and is also on a working holiday in Ireland

reached out,

so I went out for drinks!

By the way, it was her birthday!

Then,

while checking Instagram,

I noticed that a German girl

who was at the morning walking event

was nearby.

I knew the two of them were friends,

so I invited her to join us.

#Both of them had come to last week's event

She's super spontaneous, so she showed up within 20 minutes! lol

#People who come to my events are always ready to go

The three of us cheered!

We ended up drinking

until about 11:30 PM. lol

So yeah,

from morning to night, it was a day of nonstop socializing.

Even while running all these social events,

long-term Dublin residents kept inviting me

to drinks and events,

"If you invite MaSU, he'll show up."

"If you go to MaSU's events,

something interesting always happens."

I'm gradually building

that kind of reputation.

I've been making full use of these precious last 2 months of my 20s,

and I'm slowly building "trust."

In Japan, there are already lots of people

building local communities.

If I tried to do the same thing

on their turf,

I'm not rooted in any particular region right now,

so I can't build a local community.

On the flip side, my strengths are

the ability to move quickly both domestically and internationally,

and through niche combinations like

masu x photography x AI x events,

I'm building a FOMUS community overseas.

When I return to Japan,

by combining the overseas FOMUS community

with the domestic FOMUS community

and local communities,

I think we can create something even more exciting.

In Ireland,

I've found that there's almost no one

organizing international exchange meetups

as a Japanese organizer,

so I'll solidify this position.

With that, I'm charging through week 9 at full speed!

excerpt_en: So, it's Monday of my 9th week in Ireland. At this point, I had a class change. Simply put, I moved up to the intermediate class. To be honest, the things I want to say, I can more or less communicate through my daily meetup experiences,
body_en:
So, it's Monday

of my 9th week in Ireland.

At this point, I had a class change.

Simply put,

I moved up to the intermediate class.

To be honest,

the things I want to say,

I can more or less communicate

through my daily meetup experiences.

That said, personally,

my textbook English is really bad.

I talk with a "as long as I'm understood, it's fine!" attitude, so from an English teacher's perspective, that's a no-go. lol

So yeah, I finally made it to intermediate! lol

What changed was

the environment around me.

Now more than ever,

I'm in a class with people who have higher English skills.

That means

communication is much easier!

My determination to communicate is stronger than anyone's,

probably because I've talked

with so many people already.

That said, my listening skills

are still lacking,

so I'll keep working on improving my English!

After class,

I headed to my usual Starbucks

and cranked through work.

There's always something to do

when you've started your own company or brand,

so I just have to keep at it.

That's something

that's hard for others to truly understand.

But it's not like

anyone's forcing me to do it,

so it's not painful.

It's just a lot of work.

And so, I keep pushing forward!

excerpt_en: This is my first post in 2 days. Normally I write something every day, but I couldn't update. The reason: I was down with illness. Since this is my personal account, I'll be writing some raw details, and some descriptions might be unpleasant,
body_en:
This is my first post in 2 days.

Normally,

I write something every day,

but I couldn't update.

The reason: "I was down with illness."

Since this is my personal account,

I'll be writing some raw details,

and some descriptions might be unpleasant,

so feel free to skip those parts.

On the 24th,

I felt something slightly off with my body,

but I still attended class at school.

Afterward, I joined an online liberal arts lecture by Masa-san, someone I'm close with back in Japan.

That's where I realized I need to dig deeper into anime culture and history.

I'm currently in the middle of researching various things.

Everything was fine up to that point,

but the discomfort I'd felt during class

gradually grew stronger.

My body felt incredibly heavy...

After 29 years of living,

I can immediately tell when something's off with my body.

Ah, this is a bad one.

I was on the bus heading home,

but Irish buses are packed with people.

For someone feeling sick, the air was suffocating.

I started feeling nauseous mid-ride,

and even though I was still a bit far from home, I got off early.

Walking was no problem,

so I breathed in fresh air as I walked home.

By this point I could feel I had a fever,

so I went straight to bed.

But I couldn't fall asleep easily.

I could clearly tell that some powerful virus had gotten into my system.

This is rough...

The nausea kept getting worse,

and I couldn't hold it in anymore -- I threw up.

In the toilet, of course.

Anemia, vomiting, diarrhea, and high fever --

these hit me all at once about once every two years.

Since I'd been through the same thing before,

I knew how to handle it.

Once I threw up, the nausea went away,

so I hydrated

and went to sleep.

That said, I'd gone to bed super early,

so I kept waking up.

Honestly, at times like this,

I'm in no state to do any work,

so as a break I read some BORUTO manga.

Manga is my go-to when I'm sick. lol

The next morning,

I felt relatively okay, and it was card game event day, so I headed to school.

I was a bit scared to take the bus,

so I decided to walk.

*About a 45-minute walk

At this point,

I knew I still had a slight fever.

During class, honestly,

I couldn't think straight at all.

I was dizzy and spacey the whole time,

and I didn't learn a thing.

So I left during the break.

At that point,

I determined that hosting the card game event

was basically impossible,

so I canceled it.

*Even if I could hold it,

explaining SILVA in this condition would be way too hard

I took the bus to a supermarket near my house.

There, I picked up food that's good for when you're sick.

Apple juice, bananas, yogurt, water.

I hadn't eaten anything for a full day,

and I did have an appetite, so I also bought a frozen curry.

After eating,

I went straight to sleep.

Again that day, my fever kept spiking.

The virus inside me was going wild,

and my body was cranking up the heat to kill it.

Fever goes up to kill the germs,

so I needed it to finish the job quickly.

I went to bed around 5 PM that day.

I wasn't in any condition to check emails either.

At times like this,

being alone overseas is really tough.

*Even though it's a share house,

I'm the only Japanese person, so I can't trouble anyone

Day 3

And so, day 3 of feeling sick.

I'd recovered to some extent,

but still had a fever,

so I reluctantly skipped school.

If I pushed myself here,

I felt it would affect tomorrow and the whole weekend.

Better to use one day for recovery

than to struggle through three days.

So,

after eating lunch,

I went to the library near my house.

The library has Wi-Fi, which is convenient.

It was my first time visiting,

and it was very clean.

Here, I powered through all the work that had piled up over 3 days.

There were tons of emails too.

By this point, the fever was gone,

and I steadily worked through everything.

Once again, I'm reminded that I'm supported by so many people and have projects to manage, so when I go down,

it causes trouble for a lot of people...

This time, I didn't tell

any of my work colleagues that I was sick.

Telling them wouldn't bring help,

and I didn't want to worry them unnecessarily.

From the outside, it probably just seemed like

"MaSU's update frequency has dropped a bit."

And I think that's fine.

My role, as the leader of FOMUS,

is to build an environment where everyone can have more fun.

My illness has no place in that.

As for this bout of illness,

I think it came from the stress of moving for the 4th time,

the share house room being different from what I expected,

and on top of that, not having a single day of proper rest in the roughly 3 months since leaving Japan.

Especially since coming to Ireland,

I'd been going full throttle the entire time,

and the sleep deprivation and physical exhaustion

were greater than ever before.

Sleep deprivation was probably the main cause.

Over these 3 days, I got a lot of sleep,

so starting tomorrow I should be able to get back to my usual activities.

Onward and upward!

excerpt_en: Friday. Back at school after a day off. When I got there, surprisingly few people were in class. About 30% of the class was apparently sick. It seems the changing Irish climate took a lot of people down. It wasn't just me.
body_en:
Friday.

Back at school after a day off.

When I got there, surprisingly few people were in class.

About 30% of the class was apparently sick.

It seems the changing Irish climate

took a lot of people down.

It wasn't just me.

After class,

I went to check out the Starbucks where a friend had started working.

Sure enough, she was there.

That said, I had work to do too,

so I quickly grabbed a coffee and headed upstairs.

There, I just worked away.

My current share house isn't the kind of environment

where I feel like working,

so cafes and school are more productive.

Then, 7 PM.

Today, someone who's been in Ireland for about 3 years

and works in Dublin

invited me to a steak restaurant.

We hadn't really had a chance

to talk one-on-one,

so we had a nice, relaxed conversation.

I was still recovering,

but I thoroughly enjoyed some good steak and good wine

and recharged my energy!

Then, out of nowhere,

my companion's friend

invited us to go stargazing,

so I tagged along! lol

*I love when things flow like this

We drove for about 20 minutes

up into the mountains.

Obviously, there were zero streetlights.

The stars were beautifully visible.

That said, with an iPhone,

capturing stars clearly is tough. lol

While gazing at the stars,

I had an online meeting with someone in Japan.

Early morning in Japan, late night in Ireland --

a FOMUS meeting.

It was a strange feeling. lol

And finally, they drove me home.

All in all,

I'm really glad I made the decision 2 days earlier to focus on resting properly.

If I had pushed myself and gone to school

or held events in that condition,

I don't know if I would have been able to enjoy this day.

Taking proper rest is really important!
